content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

February 21, 1991

Mr. Glenn A. Miller
P.O. Box 1676
Fair Oaks, CA 95628

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-91-066

Dear Mr. Miller:

This is in response to your letter of January 18, 1991 to
Alice Hughes of the Commission's Technical Assistance and Analysis
Division, requesting advice concerning your responsibilities under
the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the "Act").¹ Because no specific governmental decision is
pending this advice is informal only.²

QUESTION

You have inquired whether your employment with the Fair Oaks
Water District (the district) would create a conflict of interest
if you were elected to the position of district director.

CONCLUSION

The Act does not prohibit you from seeking election to the
position of director for the Fair Oaks Water District. However,
because other legal provisions could affect your situation, you
should contact the district's attorney or the State Attorney
General's Office as to those matters, as discussed in this letter.

FACTS

You are contemplating seeking the elective office of director
of the Fair Oaks Water District. You have been an employee of the
district for eighteen years. Until recently, you held the
position of director of the San Juan Suburban Water District.

___________________________

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Our File No. I-91-066
Page 2

ANALYSIS

Every employee of a local government agency is considered a
"public official" for purposes of the Act. (Section 82048.)
Under the facts in this instance, by definition this includes your
present employment with the district and would also include your
contemplated position as an elected district board member. A
public official is prohibited from making, participating in, or
using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official is financially interested.
(Section 87100.)

Although public officials must generally refrain from
participating in decisions which will have a material financial
effect on sources of income to them (Section 87103(c)), salary
from a federal, state, or local government agency is not "income"
under the Act. (Section 82030(b)(2).) Thus, neither your present
salary as a district employee, nor any salary received as a
district board member, would give rise to a conflict of interest
under Section 87103(c). Therefore, the Act would not prevent you
from participating in governmental decisions upon election to the
district board despite your district employment.

Your status as a former elected director of the San Juan
Suburban Water District is not relevant for purposes of the Act's
application to the question you have raised in your letter of
January 18, 1991.

The advice provided by the Fair Political Practices
Commission is limited to the provisions of the Act. Whether other
legal provisions, such as Government Code Section 1090, the
general doctrine of incompatible offices, or any local ordinances
may have some bearing on your situation are beyond the scope of
this letter. For advice as to these matters you should contact
either the district's counsel or the state Attorney General's
Office.

I trust this letter has provided you with the guidance you
requested. If you have any further questions regarding this
matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Jonathan S. Rothman
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JSR:ken

Enclosures
